revision.text:
{{Referencing style|date=May 2021}} {{Museum | name = Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall | native_name = 和倉温泉お祭り会館 | native_name_lang = ja | image = | former_name = Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall | focus = Folklore, History | director = | owner = [[Nanao City]] | operator = Nanao-Nakanoto DMO | established = [[June 1, 2020]] | location = 13-1 Wakuramachi 2-bu, Nanao City, [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|5|11.67|N|136|55|7.49|E|display=inline,title}} | website = https://wakura-omatsuri.com/ }}
'''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall''' (和倉温泉お祭り会館, ''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Kaikan'') is a cultural museum in [[Nanao City]], [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan. Located in the famous [[Wakura Onsen]] hot spring area, the museum showcases the city's four major traditional festivals, including large-scale {{ill|Yatai (festival floats)|ja|山車}} and other festival artifacts.
== Overview == Nanao City, known for its numerous traditional festivals, decided to establish the museum following the [[2016]] inclusion of the {{ill|Seihakusai Hikiyama Festival|ja|青柏祭}} as a [[UNESCO]] [[Intangible Cultural Heritage]]. The facility was developed by renovating the aging 1981 Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall, addressing structural issues and converting the main hall into an exhibition space.<ref name="Hokkoku">Hokkoku Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Hokuriku">Hokuriku Chunichi Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Furusato">[https://www.furusato-tax.jp/gcf/493 Furusato Tax Project for Festival Preservation]</ref>
The facility highlights four key festivals: * [[Seihakusai]] - designated as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property * [[Okuma Kabuto Festival]] - also a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property * [[Mukōda Fire Festival]] - recognized as a [[Prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] and part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals" * [[Ishizaki Hōtō Festival]] - part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
Initially slated for an April 25, 2020, opening, the launch was delayed due to the COVID-19 pandemic.<ref name="Hokkoku"/>
== Features == ### Exhibition Hall (Paid Admission) The former 1,100-seat main hall has been transformed into a 17-meter-high exhibition space displaying full-scale festival floats and artifacts.<ref>[http://www.ryoko-net.co.jp/?p=78090 Ryoko Shimbun]</ref> Exhibits include: * Seihakusai's "Dekayama" - a new full-scale replica created as a fourth float for permanent display. * Okuma Kabuto's Waku Bata - 16 meters high. * Mukōda Fire Festival’s Ōtaimatsu - represented by a 15-meter banner due to the original’s 30-meter height. * Ishizaki Hōtō’s Daifutō - 13 meters high.
### Festival Theater and Interactive Zone The theater features a 16.2-meter-wide screen showing immersive festival footage. Interactive experiences allow visitors to "participate" in festivals through projections and simulations.<ref name="Hokuriku"/>
### Tourism Information Entrance (Free Zone) The entrance area features: * Miniature and small-scale exhibits of local floats. * Tourism information and guides. * A gift shop offering festival-themed souvenirs.
### Additional Facilities * Meeting rooms and Japanese-style rooms (available for rent). * Cooking practice rooms.
== Visitor Information == * Open: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM (last entry at 4:30 PM) * Closed: Second and fourth Wednesdays (or following day if a holiday) and during New Year’s holidays. * Admission: Adults ¥800, children ¥400, infants free. * Free parking available for buses and private vehicles.
== Access == * '''By car''': ~6 minutes from [[Wakura IC]] on the [[Noto Satoyama Kaidō]]. * '''By train''': ~20 minutes on foot or 6 minutes by car from [[Wakura Onsen Station]]. * '''By bus''': Immediate access from "Omatsuri Kaikan" bus stop (Hokutetsu Noto Bus).
== Surrounding Attractions == * [[Wakura Onsen]] * Wakura Onsen Sōyu Public Bath * [[Wakura Showa Museum and Toy Museum]] * [[Isolite Diatomite Memorial Museum]] * [[Yoshihiro Tsujiguchi Art Museum]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * {{Official website|https://wakura-omatsuri.com/|Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall}}

revision.text:
{{Referencing style|date=May 2021}} {{Museum | name = Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall | native_name = 和倉温泉お祭り会館 | native_name_lang = ja | image = | former_name = Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall | focus = Folklore, History | director = | owner = [[Nanao City]] | operator = Nanao-Nakanoto DMO | established = [[June 1, 2020]] | location = 13-1 Wakuramachi 2-bu, Nanao City, [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|5|11.67|N|136|55|7.49|E|display=inline,title}} | website = https://wakura-omatsuri.com/ }}
'''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall''' (和倉温泉お祭り会館, ''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Kaikan'') is a cultural museum in [[Nanao City]], [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan. Located in the famous [[Wakura Onsen]] hot spring area, the museum showcases the city's four major traditional festivals, including large-scale {{ill|Yatai (festival floats)|ja|山車}} and other festival artifacts.
== Overview == Nanao City, known for its numerous traditional festivals, decided to establish the museum following the [[2016]] inclusion of the {{ill|Seihakusai Hikiyama Festival|ja|青柏祭}} as a [[UNESCO]] [[Intangible Cultural Heritage]]. The facility was developed by renovating the aging 1981 Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall, addressing structural issues and converting the main hall into an exhibition space.<ref name="Hokkoku">Hokkoku Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Hokuriku">Hokuriku Chunichi Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Furusato">[https://www.furusato-tax.jp/gcf/493 Furusato Tax Project for Festival Preservation]</ref>
The facility highlights four key festivals: * [[Seihakusai]] - designated as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property * [[Okuma Kabuto Festival]] - also a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property * [[Mukōda Fire Festival]] - recognized as a [[Prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] and part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals" * [[Ishizaki Hōtō Festival]] - part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
Initially slated for an April 25, 2020, opening, the launch was delayed due to the COVID-19 pandemic.<ref name="Hokkoku"/>
== Features == ### Exhibition Hall (Paid Admission) The former 1,100-seat main hall has been transformed into a 17-meter-high exhibition space displaying full-scale festival floats and artifacts.<ref>[http://www.ryoko-net.co.jp/?p=78090 Ryoko Shimbun]</ref> Exhibits include: * Seihakusai's "Dekayama" - a new full-scale replica created as a fourth float for permanent display. * Okuma Kabuto's Waku Bata - 16 meters high. * Mukōda Fire Festival’s Ōtaimatsu - represented by a 15-meter banner due to the original’s 30-meter height. * Ishizaki Hōtō’s Daifutō - 13 meters high.
### Festival Theater and Interactive Zone The theater features a 16.2-meter-wide screen showing immersive festival footage. Interactive experiences allow visitors to "participate" in festivals through projections and simulations.<ref name="Hokuriku"/>
### Tourism Information Entrance (Free Zone) The entrance area features: * Miniature and small-scale exhibits of local floats. * Tourism information and guides. * A gift shop offering festival-themed souvenirs.
### Additional Facilities * Meeting rooms and Japanese-style rooms (available for rent). * Cooking practice rooms.
== Visitor Information == * Open: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM (last entry at 4:30 PM) * Closed: Second and fourth Wednesdays (or following day if a holiday) and during New Year’s holidays. * Admission: Adults ¥800, children ¥400, infants free. * Free parking available for buses and private vehicles.
== Access == * '''By car''': ~6 minutes from [[Wakura IC]] on the [[Noto Satoyama Kaidō]]. * '''By train''': ~20 minutes on foot or 6 minutes by car from [[Wakura Onsen Station]]. * '''By bus''': Immediate access from "Omatsuri Kaikan" bus stop (Hokutetsu Noto Bus).
== Surrounding Attractions == * [[Wakura Onsen]] * Wakura Onsen Sōyu Public Bath * [[Wakura Showa Museum and Toy Museum]] * [[Isolite Diatomite Memorial Museum]] * [[Yoshihiro Tsujiguchi Art Museum]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * {{Official website|https://wakura-omatsuri.com/|Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall}}

== Features == ### Exhibition Hall (Paid Admission) The former 1,100-seat main hall has been transformed into a 17-meter-high exhibition space displaying full-scale festival floats and artifacts.<ref>[http://www.ryoko-net.co.jp/?p=78090 Ryoko Shimbun]</ref> Exhibits include: * Seihakusai's "Dekayama" - a new full-scale replica created as a fourth float for permanent display. * Okuma Kabuto's Waku Bata - 16 meters high. * Mukōda Fire Festival’s Ōtaimatsu - represented by a 15-meter banner due to the original’s 30-meter height. * Ishizaki Hōtō’s Daifutō - 13 meters high.
### Festival Theater and Interactive Zone The theater features a 16.2-meter-wide screen showing immersive festival footage. Interactive experiences allow visitors to "participate" in festivals through projections and simulations.<ref name="Hokuriku"/>
### Tourism Information Entrance (Free Zone) The entrance area features: * Miniature and small-scale exhibits of local floats. * Tourism information and guides. * A gift shop offering festival-themed souvenirs.
### Additional Facilities * Meeting rooms and Japanese-style rooms (available for rent). * Cooking practice rooms.
== Visitor Information == * Open: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM (last entry at 4:30 PM) * Closed: Second and fourth Wednesdays (or following day if a holiday) and during New Year’s holidays. * Admission: Adults ¥800, children ¥400, infants free. * Free parking available for buses and private vehicles.
== Access == * '''By car''': ~6 minutes from [[Wakura IC]] on the [[Noto Satoyama Kaidō]]. * '''By train''': ~20 minutes on foot or 6 minutes by car from [[Wakura Onsen Station]]. * '''By bus''': Immediate access from "Omatsuri Kaikan" bus stop (Hokutetsu Noto Bus).
== Surrounding Attractions == * [[Wakura Onsen]] * Wakura Onsen Sōyu Public Bath * [[Wakura Showa Museum and Toy Museum]] * [[Isolite Diatomite Memorial Museum]] * [[Yoshihiro Tsujiguchi Art Museum]]

The facility highlights four key festivals: * [[Seihakusai]] - designated as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property * [[Okuma Kabuto Festival]] - also a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property * [[Mukōda Fire Festival]] - recognized as a [[Prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] and part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals" * [[Ishizaki Hōtō Festival]] - part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"

### Tourism Information Entrance (Free Zone) The entrance area features: * Miniature and small-scale exhibits of local floats. * Tourism information and guides. * A gift shop offering festival-themed souvenirs.
### Additional Facilities * Meeting rooms and Japanese-style rooms (available for rent). * Cooking practice rooms.
== Visitor Information == * Open: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM (last entry at 4:30 PM) * Closed: Second and fourth Wednesdays (or following day if a holiday) and during New Year’s holidays. * Admission: Adults ¥800, children ¥400, infants free. * Free parking available for buses and private vehicles.

revision.text:
{{Referencing style|date=May 2021}} {{Museum | name = Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall | native_name = 和倉温泉お祭り会館 | native_name_lang = ja | image = | former_name = Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall | focus = Folklore, History | director = | owner = [[Nanao City]] | operator = Nanao-Nakanoto DMO | established = [[June 1, 2020]] | location = 13-1 Wakuramachi 2-bu, Nanao City, [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|5|11.67|N|136|55|7.49|E|display=inline,title}} | website = https://wakura-omatsuri.com/ }}
'''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall''' (和倉温泉お祭り会館, ''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Kaikan'') is a cultural museum in [[Nanao City]], [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan. Located in the famous [[Wakura Onsen]] hot spring area, the museum showcases the city's four major traditional festivals, including large-scale {{ill|Yatai (festival floats)|ja|山車}} and other festival artifacts.
== Overview == Nanao City, known for its numerous traditional festivals, decided to establish the museum following the [[2016]] inclusion of the {{ill|Seihakusai Hikiyama Festival|ja|青柏祭}} as a [[UNESCO]] [[Intangible Cultural Heritage]]. The facility was developed by renovating the aging 1981 Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall, addressing structural issues and converting the main hall into an exhibition space.<ref name="Hokkoku">Hokkoku Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Hokuriku">Hokuriku Chunichi Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Furusato">[https://www.furusato-tax.jp/gcf/493 Furusato Tax Project for Festival Preservation]</ref>
The facility highlights four key festivals: * [[Seihakusai]] - designated as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property * [[Okuma Kabuto Festival]] - also a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property * [[Mukōda Fire Festival]] - recognized as a [[Prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] and part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals" * [[Ishizaki Hōtō Festival]] - part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
Initially slated for an April 25, 2020, opening, the launch was delayed due to the COVID-19 pandemic.<ref name="Hokkoku"/>
== Features == ### Exhibition Hall (Paid Admission) The former 1,100-seat main hall has been transformed into a 17-meter-high exhibition space displaying full-scale festival floats and artifacts.<ref>[http://www.ryoko-net.co.jp/?p=78090 Ryoko Shimbun]</ref> Exhibits include: * Seihakusai's "Dekayama" - a new full-scale replica created as a fourth float for permanent display. * Okuma Kabuto's Waku Bata - 16 meters high. * Mukōda Fire Festival’s Ōtaimatsu - represented by a 15-meter banner due to the original’s 30-meter height. * Ishizaki Hōtō’s Daifutō - 13 meters high.
### Festival Theater and Interactive Zone The theater features a 16.2-meter-wide screen showing immersive festival footage. Interactive experiences allow visitors to "participate" in festivals through projections and simulations.<ref name="Hokuriku"/>
### Tourism Information Entrance (Free Zone) The entrance area features: * Miniature and small-scale exhibits of local floats. * Tourism information and guides. * A gift shop offering festival-themed souvenirs.
### Additional Facilities * Meeting rooms and Japanese-style rooms (available for rent). * Cooking practice rooms.
== Visitor Information == * Open: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM (last entry at 4:30 PM) * Closed: Second and fourth Wednesdays (or following day if a holiday) and during New Year’s holidays. * Admission: Adults ¥800, children ¥400, infants free. * Free parking available for buses and private vehicles.
== Access == * '''By car''': ~6 minutes from [[Wakura IC]] on the [[Noto Satoyama Kaidō]]. * '''By train''': ~20 minutes on foot or 6 minutes by car from [[Wakura Onsen Station]]. * '''By bus''': Immediate access from "Omatsuri Kaikan" bus stop (Hokutetsu Noto Bus).
== Surrounding Attractions == * [[Wakura Onsen]] * Wakura Onsen Sōyu Public Bath * [[Wakura Showa Museum and Toy Museum]] * [[Isolite Diatomite Memorial Museum]] * [[Yoshihiro Tsujiguchi Art Museum]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * {{Official website|https://wakura-omatsuri.com/|Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall}}

revision.text:
{{Referencing style|date=May 2021}} {{Museum | name = Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall | native_name = 和倉温泉お祭り会館 | native_name_lang = ja | image = | former_name = Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall | focus = Folklore, History | director = | owner = [[Nanao City]] | operator = Nanao-Nakanoto DMO | established = [[June 1, 2020]] | location = 13-1 Wakuramachi 2-bu, Nanao City, [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|5|11.67|N|136|55|7.49|E|display=inline,title}} | website = https://wakura-omatsuri.com/ }}
'''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall''' (和倉温泉お祭り会館, ''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Kaikan'') is a cultural museum in [[Nanao City]], [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan. Located in the famous [[Wakura Onsen]] hot spring area, the museum showcases the city's four major traditional festivals, including large-scale {{ill|Yatai (festival floats)|ja|山車}} and other festival artifacts.
== Overview == Nanao City, known for its numerous traditional festivals, decided to establish the museum following the [[2016]] inclusion of the {{ill|Seihakusai Hikiyama Festival|ja|青柏祭}} as a [[UNESCO]] [[Intangible Cultural Heritage]]. The facility was developed by renovating the aging 1981 Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall, addressing structural issues and converting the main hall into an exhibition space.<ref name="Hokkoku">Hokkoku Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Hokuriku">Hokuriku Chunichi Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Furusato">[https://www.furusato-tax.jp/gcf/493 Furusato Tax Project for Festival Preservation]</ref>
The facility highlights four key festivals: * [[Seihakusai]] - designated as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property * [[Okuma Kabuto Festival]] - also a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property * [[Mukōda Fire Festival]] - recognized as a [[Prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] and part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals" * [[Ishizaki Hōtō Festival]] - part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
Initially slated for an April 25, 2020, opening, the launch was delayed due to the COVID-19 pandemic.<ref name="Hokkoku"/>
== Features == ### Exhibition Hall (Paid Admission) The former 1,100-seat main hall has been transformed into a 17-meter-high exhibition space displaying full-scale festival floats and artifacts.<ref>[http://www.ryoko-net.co.jp/?p=78090 Ryoko Shimbun]</ref> Exhibits include: * Seihakusai's "Dekayama" - a new full-scale replica created as a fourth float for permanent display. * Okuma Kabuto's Waku Bata - 16 meters high. * Mukōda Fire Festival’s Ōtaimatsu - represented by a 15-meter banner due to the original’s 30-meter height. * Ishizaki Hōtō’s Daifutō - 13 meters high.
### Festival Theater and Interactive Zone The theater features a 16.2-meter-wide screen showing immersive festival footage. Interactive experiences allow visitors to "participate" in festivals through projections and simulations.<ref name="Hokuriku"/>
### Tourism Information Entrance (Free Zone) The entrance area features: * Miniature and small-scale exhibits of local floats. * Tourism information and guides. * A gift shop offering festival-themed souvenirs.
### Additional Facilities * Meeting rooms and Japanese-style rooms (available for rent). * Cooking practice rooms.
== Visitor Information == * Open: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM (last entry at 4:30 PM) * Closed: Second and fourth Wednesdays (or following day if a holiday) and during New Year’s holidays. * Admission: Adults ¥800, children ¥400, infants free. * Free parking available for buses and private vehicles.
== Access == * '''By car''': ~6 minutes from [[Wakura IC]] on the [[Noto Satoyama Kaidō]]. * '''By train''': ~20 minutes on foot or 6 minutes by car from [[Wakura Onsen Station]]. * '''By bus''': Immediate access from "Omatsuri Kaikan" bus stop (Hokutetsu Noto Bus).
== Surrounding Attractions == * [[Wakura Onsen]] * Wakura Onsen Sōyu Public Bath * [[Wakura Showa Museum and Toy Museum]] * [[Isolite Diatomite Memorial Museum]] * [[Yoshihiro Tsujiguchi Art Museum]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * {{Official website|https://wakura-omatsuri.com/|Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall}}

### Additional Facilities * Meeting rooms and Japanese-style rooms (available for rent). * Cooking practice rooms.
== Visitor Information == * Open: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM (last entry at 4:30 PM) * Closed: Second and fourth Wednesdays (or following day if a holiday) and during New Year’s holidays. * Admission: Adults ¥800, children ¥400, infants free. * Free parking available for buses and private vehicles.
== Access == * '''By car''': ~6 minutes from [[Wakura IC]] on the [[Noto Satoyama Kaidō]]. * '''By train''': ~20 minutes on foot or 6 minutes by car from [[Wakura Onsen Station]]. * '''By bus''': Immediate access from "Omatsuri Kaikan" bus stop (Hokutetsu Noto Bus).
== Surrounding Attractions == * [[Wakura Onsen]] * Wakura Onsen Sōyu Public Bath * [[Wakura Showa Museum and Toy Museum]] * [[Isolite Diatomite Memorial Museum]] * [[Yoshihiro Tsujiguchi Art Museum]]

revision.text:
{{Referencing style|date=May 2021}} {{Museum | name = Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall | native_name = 和倉温泉お祭り会館 | native_name_lang = ja | image = | former_name = Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall | focus = Folklore, History | director = | owner = [[Nanao City]] | operator = Nanao-Nakanoto DMO | established = [[June 1, 2020]] | location = 13-1 Wakuramachi 2-bu, Nanao City, [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan | coordinates = {{coord|37|5|11.67|N|136|55|7.49|E|display=inline,title}} | website = https://wakura-omatsuri.com/ }}
'''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall''' (和倉温泉お祭り会館, ''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Kaikan'') is a cultural museum in [[Nanao City]], [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan. Located in the famous [[Wakura Onsen]] hot spring area, the museum showcases the city's four major traditional festivals, including large-scale {{ill|Yatai (festival floats)|ja|山車}} and other festival artifacts.
== Overview == Nanao City, known for its numerous traditional festivals, decided to establish the museum following the [[2016]] inclusion of the {{ill|Seihakusai Hikiyama Festival|ja|青柏祭}} as a [[UNESCO]] [[Intangible Cultural Heritage]]. The facility was developed by renovating the aging 1981 Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall, addressing structural issues and converting the main hall into an exhibition space.<ref name="Hokkoku">Hokkoku Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Hokuriku">Hokuriku Chunichi Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Furusato">[https://www.furusato-tax.jp/gcf/493 Furusato Tax Project for Festival Preservation]</ref>
The facility highlights four key festivals: * [[Seihakusai]] - designated as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property * [[Okuma Kabuto Festival]] - also a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property * [[Mukōda Fire Festival]] - recognized as a [[Prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] and part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals" * [[Ishizaki Hōtō Festival]] - part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
Initially slated for an April 25, 2020, opening, the launch was delayed due to the COVID-19 pandemic.<ref name="Hokkoku"/>
== Features == ### Exhibition Hall (Paid Admission) The former 1,100-seat main hall has been transformed into a 17-meter-high exhibition space displaying full-scale festival floats and artifacts.<ref>[http://www.ryoko-net.co.jp/?p=78090 Ryoko Shimbun]</ref> Exhibits include: * Seihakusai's "Dekayama" - a new full-scale replica created as a fourth float for permanent display. * Okuma Kabuto's Waku Bata - 16 meters high. * Mukōda Fire Festival’s Ōtaimatsu - represented by a 15-meter banner due to the original’s 30-meter height. * Ishizaki Hōtō’s Daifutō - 13 meters high.
### Festival Theater and Interactive Zone The theater features a 16.2-meter-wide screen showing immersive festival footage. Interactive experiences allow visitors to "participate" in festivals through projections and simulations.<ref name="Hokuriku"/>
### Tourism Information Entrance (Free Zone) The entrance area features: * Miniature and small-scale exhibits of local floats. * Tourism information and guides. * A gift shop offering festival-themed souvenirs.
### Additional Facilities * Meeting rooms and Japanese-style rooms (available for rent). * Cooking practice rooms.
== Visitor Information == * Open: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM (last entry at 4:30 PM) * Closed: Second and fourth Wednesdays (or following day if a holiday) and during New Year’s holidays. * Admission: Adults ¥800, children ¥400, infants free. * Free parking available for buses and private vehicles.
== Access == * '''By car''': ~6 minutes from [[Wakura IC]] on the [[Noto Satoyama Kaidō]]. * '''By train''': ~20 minutes on foot or 6 minutes by car from [[Wakura Onsen Station]]. * '''By bus''': Immediate access from "Omatsuri Kaikan" bus stop (Hokutetsu Noto Bus).
== Surrounding Attractions == * [[Wakura Onsen]] * Wakura Onsen Sōyu Public Bath * [[Wakura Showa Museum and Toy Museum]] * [[Isolite Diatomite Memorial Museum]] * [[Yoshihiro Tsujiguchi Art Museum]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * {{Official website|https://wakura-omatsuri.com/|Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall}}

'''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall''' (和倉温泉お祭り会館, ''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Kaikan'') is a cultural museum in [[Nanao City]], [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan. Located in the famous [[Wakura Onsen]] hot spring area, the museum showcases the city's four major traditional festivals, including large-scale {{ill|Yatai (festival floats)|ja|山車}} and other festival artifacts.
== Overview == Nanao City, known for its numerous traditional festivals, decided to establish the museum following the [[2016]] inclusion of the {{ill|Seihakusai Hikiyama Festival|ja|青柏祭}} as a [[UNESCO]] [[Intangible Cultural Heritage]]. The facility was developed by renovating the aging 1981 Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall, addressing structural issues and converting the main hall into an exhibition space.<ref name="Hokkoku">Hokkoku Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Hokuriku">Hokuriku Chunichi Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Furusato">[https://www.furusato-tax.jp/gcf/493 Furusato Tax Project for Festival Preservation]</ref>
The facility highlights four key festivals: * [[Seihakusai]] - designated as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property * [[Okuma Kabuto Festival]] - also a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property * [[Mukōda Fire Festival]] - recognized as a [[Prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] and part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals" * [[Ishizaki Hōtō Festival]] - part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
Initially slated for an April 25, 2020, opening, the launch was delayed due to the COVID-19 pandemic.<ref name="Hokkoku"/>
== Features == ### Exhibition Hall (Paid Admission) The former 1,100-seat main hall has been transformed into a 17-meter-high exhibition space displaying full-scale festival floats and artifacts.<ref>[http://www.ryoko-net.co.jp/?p=78090 Ryoko Shimbun]</ref> Exhibits include: * Seihakusai's "Dekayama" - a new full-scale replica created as a fourth float for permanent display. * Okuma Kabuto's Waku Bata - 16 meters high. * Mukōda Fire Festival’s Ōtaimatsu - represented by a 15-meter banner due to the original’s 30-meter height. * Ishizaki Hōtō’s Daifutō - 13 meters high.
### Festival Theater and Interactive Zone The theater features a 16.2-meter-wide screen showing immersive festival footage. Interactive experiences allow visitors to "participate" in festivals through projections and simulations.<ref name="Hokuriku"/>
### Tourism Information Entrance (Free Zone) The entrance area features: * Miniature and small-scale exhibits of local floats. * Tourism information and guides. * A gift shop offering festival-themed souvenirs.
### Additional Facilities * Meeting rooms and Japanese-style rooms (available for rent). * Cooking practice rooms.
== Visitor Information == * Open: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM (last entry at 4:30 PM) * Closed: Second and fourth Wednesdays (or following day if a holiday) and during New Year’s holidays. * Admission: Adults ¥800, children ¥400, infants free. * Free parking available for buses and private vehicles.
== Access == * '''By car''': ~6 minutes from [[Wakura IC]] on the [[Noto Satoyama Kaidō]]. * '''By train''': ~20 minutes on foot or 6 minutes by car from [[Wakura Onsen Station]]. * '''By bus''': Immediate access from "Omatsuri Kaikan" bus stop (Hokutetsu Noto Bus).
== Surrounding Attractions == * [[Wakura Onsen]] * Wakura Onsen Sōyu Public Bath * [[Wakura Showa Museum and Toy Museum]] * [[Isolite Diatomite Memorial Museum]] * [[Yoshihiro Tsujiguchi Art Museum]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * {{Official website|https://wakura-omatsuri.com/|Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall}}

'''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall''' (和倉温泉お祭り会館, ''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Kaikan'') is a cultural museum in [[Nanao City]], [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan. Located in the famous [[Wakura Onsen]] hot spring area, the museum showcases the city's four major traditional festivals, including large-scale {{ill|Yatai (festival floats)|ja|山車}} and other festival artifacts.
== Overview ==
Nanao City, known for its numerous traditional festivals, decided to establish the museum following the [[2016]] inclusion of the {{ill|Seihakusai Hikiyama Festival|ja|青柏祭}} as a [[UNESCO]] [[Intangible Cultural Heritage]]. The facility was developed by renovating the aging 1981 Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall, addressing structural issues and converting the main hall into an exhibition space.<ref name="Hokkoku">Hokkoku Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Hokuriku">Hokuriku Chunichi Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Furusato">[https://www.furusato-tax.jp/gcf/493 Furusato Tax Project for Festival Preservation]</ref>
The facility highlights four key festivals:
* [[Seihakusai]] - designated as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Okuma Kabuto Festival]] - also a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Mukōda Fire Festival]] - recognized as a [[Prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] and part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
* [[Ishizaki Hōtō Festival]] - part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
Initially slated for an April 25, 2020, opening, the launch was delayed due to the COVID-19 pandemic.<ref name="Hokkoku"/>
== Features ==
=== Exhibition Hall (Paid Admission)
The former 1,100-seat main hall has been transformed into a 17-meter-high exhibition space displaying full-scale festival floats and artifacts.<ref>[http://www.ryoko-net.co.jp/?p=78090 Ryoko Shimbun]</ref> Exhibits include:
* Seihakusai's "Dekayama" - a new full-scale replica created as a fourth float for permanent display.
* Okuma Kabuto's Waku Bata - 16 meters high.
* Mukōda Fire Festival’s Ōtaimatsu - represented by a 15-meter banner due to the original’s 30-meter height.
* Ishizaki Hōtō’s Daifutō - 13 meters high.
=== Festival Theater and Interactive Zone
The theater features a 16.2-meter-wide screen showing immersive festival footage. Interactive experiences allow visitors to "participate" in festivals through projections and simulations.<ref name="Hokuriku"/>
=== Tourism Information Entrance (Free Zone)
The entrance area features:
* Miniature and small-scale exhibits of local floats.
* Tourism information and guides.
* A gift shop offering festival-themed souvenirs.
=== Additional Facilities
* Meeting rooms and Japanese-style rooms (available for rent).
* Cooking practice rooms.
== Visitor Information ==
* Open: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM (last entry at 4:30 PM)
* Closed: Second and fourth Wednesdays (or following day if a holiday) and during New Year’s holidays.
* Admission: Adults ¥800, children ¥400, infants free.
* Free parking available for buses and private vehicles.
== Access ==
* '''By car''': ~6 minutes from [[Wakura IC]] on the [[Noto Satoyama Kaidō]].
* '''By train''': ~20 minutes on foot or 6 minutes by car from [[Wakura Onsen Station]].
* '''By bus''': Immediate access from "Omatsuri Kaikan" bus stop (Hokutetsu Noto Bus).
== Surrounding Attractions ==
* [[Wakura Onsen]]
* Wakura Onsen Sōyu Public Bath
* [[Wakura Showa Museum and Toy Museum]]
* [[Isolite Diatomite Memorial Museum]]
* [[Yoshihiro Tsujiguchi Art Museum]]

{{Museum
| name = Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall
| native_name = 和倉温泉お祭り会館
| native_name_lang = ja
| image =
| former_name = Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall
| focus = Folklore, History
| director =
| owner = [[Nanao City]]
| operator = Nanao-Nakanoto DMO
| established = [[June 1, 2020]]
| location = 13-1 Wakuramachi 2-bu, Nanao City, [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan
| coordinates = {{coord|37|5|11.67|N|136|55|7.49|E|display=inline,title}}
| website = https://wakura-omatsuri.com/
}}
'''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall''' (和倉温泉お祭り会館, ''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Kaikan'') is a cultural museum in [[Nanao City]], [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan. Located in the famous [[Wakura Onsen]] hot spring area, the museum showcases the city's four major traditional festivals, including large-scale {{ill|Yatai (festival floats)|ja|山車}} and other festival artifacts.
== Overview ==
Nanao City, known for its numerous traditional festivals, decided to establish the museum following the [[2016]] inclusion of the {{ill|Seihakusai Hikiyama Festival|ja|青柏祭}} as a [[UNESCO]] [[Intangible Cultural Heritage]]. The facility was developed by renovating the aging 1981 Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall, addressing structural issues and converting the main hall into an exhibition space.<ref name="Hokkoku">Hokkoku Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Hokuriku">Hokuriku Chunichi Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Furusato">[https://www.furusato-tax.jp/gcf/493 Furusato Tax Project for Festival Preservation]</ref>
The facility highlights four key festivals:
* [[Seihakusai]] - designated as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Okuma Kabuto Festival]] - also a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Mukōda Fire Festival]] - recognized as a [[Prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] and part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
* [[Ishizaki Hōtō Festival]] - part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
Initially slated for an April 25, 2020, opening, the launch was delayed due to the COVID-19 pandemic.<ref name="Hokkoku"/>
== Features ==
=== Exhibition Hall (Paid Admission)
The former 1,100-seat main hall has been transformed into a 17-meter-high exhibition space displaying full-scale festival floats and artifacts.<ref>[http://www.ryoko-net.co.jp/?p=78090 Ryoko Shimbun]</ref> Exhibits include:
* Seihakusai's "Dekayama" - a new full-scale replica created as a fourth float for permanent display.
* Okuma Kabuto's Waku Bata - 16 meters high.
* Mukōda Fire Festival’s Ōtaimatsu - represented by a 15-meter banner due to the original’s 30-meter height.
* Ishizaki Hōtō’s Daifutō - 13 meters high.
=== Festival Theater and Interactive Zone
The theater features a 16.2-meter-wide screen showing immersive festival footage. Interactive experiences allow visitors to "participate" in festivals through projections and simulations.<ref name="Hokuriku"/>
=== Tourism Information Entrance (Free Zone)
The entrance area features:
* Miniature and small-scale exhibits of local floats.
* Tourism information and guides.
* A gift shop offering festival-themed souvenirs.
=== Additional Facilities
* Meeting rooms and Japanese-style rooms (available for rent).
* Cooking practice rooms.
== Visitor Information ==
* Open: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM (last entry at 4:30 PM)
* Closed: Second and fourth Wednesdays (or following day if a holiday) and during New Year’s holidays.
* Admission: Adults ¥800, children ¥400, infants free.
* Free parking available for buses and private vehicles.
== Access ==
* '''By car''': ~6 minutes from [[Wakura IC]] on the [[Noto Satoyama Kaidō]].
* '''By train''': ~20 minutes on foot or 6 minutes by car from [[Wakura Onsen Station]].
* '''By bus''': Immediate access from "Omatsuri Kaikan" bus stop (Hokutetsu Noto Bus).
== Surrounding Attractions ==
* [[Wakura Onsen]]
* Wakura Onsen Sōyu Public Bath
* [[Wakura Showa Museum and Toy Museum]]
* [[Isolite Diatomite Memorial Museum]]
* [[Yoshihiro Tsujiguchi Art Museum]]
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== External Links ==

* [[Seihakusai]] - designated as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Okuma Kabuto Festival]] - also a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Mukōda Fire Festival]] - recognized as a [[Prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] and part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
* [[Ishizaki Hōtō Festival]] - part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
Initially slated for an April 25, 2020, opening, the launch was delayed due to the COVID-19 pandemic.<ref name="Hokkoku"/>
== Features ==
=== Exhibition Hall (Paid Admission)
The former 1,100-seat main hall has been transformed into a 17-meter-high exhibition space displaying full-scale festival floats and artifacts.<ref>[http://www.ryoko-net.co.jp/?p=78090 Ryoko Shimbun]</ref> Exhibits include:
* Seihakusai's "Dekayama" - a new full-scale replica created as a fourth float for permanent display.
* Okuma Kabuto's Waku Bata - 16 meters high.
* Mukōda Fire Festival’s Ōtaimatsu - represented by a 15-meter banner due to the original’s 30-meter height.
* Ishizaki Hōtō’s Daifutō - 13 meters high.
=== === Festival Theater and Interactive Zone === The theater features a 16.2-meter-wide screen showing immersive festival footage. Interactive experiences allow visitors to "participate" in festivals through projections and simulations.<ref name="Hokuriku"/>
=== === Tourism Information Entrance (Free Zone) === The entrance area features:
* Miniature and small-scale exhibits of local floats.
* Tourism information and guides.
* A gift shop offering festival-themed souvenirs.
=== === Additional Facilities === * Meeting rooms and Japanese-style rooms (available for rent).
* Cooking practice rooms.
== Visitor Information ==
* Open: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM (last entry at 4:30 PM)
* Closed: Second and fourth Wednesdays (or following day if a holiday) and during New Year’s holidays.
* Admission: Adults ¥800, children ¥400, infants free.
* Free parking available for buses and private vehicles.
== Access ==
* '''By car''': ~6 minutes from [[Wakura IC]] on the [[Noto Satoyama Kaidō]].
* '''By train''': ~20 minutes on foot or 6 minutes by car from [[Wakura Onsen Station]].
* '''By bus''': Immediate access from "Omatsuri Kaikan" bus stop (Hokutetsu Noto Bus).
== Surrounding Attractions ==
* [[Wakura Onsen]]
* Wakura Onsen Sōyu Public Bath
* [[Wakura Showa Museum and Toy Museum]]
* [[Isolite Diatomite Memorial Museum]]
* [[Yoshihiro Tsujiguchi Art Museum]]

'''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall''' (和倉温泉お祭り会館, ''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Kaikan'') is a cultural museum in [[Nanao City]], [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan. Located in the famous [[Wakura Onsen]] hot spring area, the museum showcases the city's four major traditional festivals, including large-scale {{ill|Yatai (festival floats)|ja|山車}} and other festival artifacts.
== Overview ==
Nanao City, known for its numerous traditional festivals, decided to establish the museum following the [[2016]] inclusion of the {{ill|Seihakusai Hikiyama Festival|ja|青柏祭}} as a [[UNESCO]] [[Intangible Cultural Heritage]]. The facility was developed by renovating the aging 1981 Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall, addressing structural issues and converting the main hall into an exhibition space.<ref name="Hokkoku">Hokkoku Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Hokuriku">Hokuriku Chunichi Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Furusato">[https://www.furusato-tax.jp/gcf/493 Furusato Tax Project for Festival Preservation]</ref>
The facility highlights four key festivals:
* [[Seihakusai]] - designated as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Okuma Kabuto Festival]] - also a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Mukōda Fire Festival]] - recognized as a [[Prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] and part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
* [[Ishizaki Hōtō Festival]] - part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
Initially slated for an April 25, 2020, opening, the launch was delayed due to the COVID-19 pandemic.<ref name="Hokkoku"/>
== Features ==
=== Exhibition Hall (Paid Admission)
The former 1,100-seat main hall has been transformed into a 17-meter-high exhibition space displaying full-scale festival floats and artifacts.<ref>[http://www.ryoko-net.co.jp/?p=78090 Ryoko Shimbun]</ref> Exhibits include:
* Seihakusai's "Dekayama" - a new full-scale replica created as a fourth float for permanent display.
* Okuma Kabuto's Waku Bata - 16 meters high.
* Mukōda Fire Festival’s Ōtaimatsu - represented by a 15-meter banner due to the original’s 30-meter height.
* Ishizaki Hōtō’s Daifutō - 13 meters high.
=== === Festival Theater and Interactive Zone === The theater features a 16.2-meter-wide screen showing immersive festival footage. Interactive experiences allow visitors to "participate" in festivals through projections and simulations.<ref name="Hokuriku"/>
=== === Tourism Information Entrance (Free Zone) === The entrance area features:
* Miniature and small-scale exhibits of local floats.
* Tourism information and guides.
* A gift shop offering festival-themed souvenirs.
=== === Additional Facilities === * Meeting rooms and Japanese-style rooms (available for rent).
* Cooking practice rooms.
== Visitor Information ==
* Open: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM (last entry at 4:30 PM)
* Closed: Second and fourth Wednesdays (or following day if a holiday) and during New Year’s holidays.
* Admission: Adults ¥800, children ¥400, infants free.
* Free parking available for buses and private vehicles.
== Access ==
* '''By car''': ~6 minutes from [[Wakura IC]] on the [[Noto Satoyama Kaidō]].
* '''By train''': ~20 minutes on foot or 6 minutes by car from [[Wakura Onsen Station]].
* '''By bus''': Immediate access from "Omatsuri Kaikan" bus stop (Hokutetsu Noto Bus).
== Surrounding Attractions ==
* [[Wakura Onsen]]
* Wakura Onsen Sōyu Public Bath
* [[Wakura Showa Museum and Toy Museum]]
* [[Isolite Diatomite Memorial Museum]]
* [[Yoshihiro Tsujiguchi Art Museum]]

revision.text:
{{Referencing style|date=May 2021}}
{{Museum
| name = Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall
| native_name = 和倉温泉お祭り会館
| native_name_lang = ja
| image =
| former_name = Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall
| focus = Folklore, History
| director =
| owner = [[Nanao City]]
| operator = Nanao-Nakanoto DMO
| established = [[June 1, 2020]]
| location = 13-1 Wakuramachi 2-bu, Nanao City, [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan
| coordinates = {{coord|37|5|11.67|N|136|55|7.49|E|display=inline,title}}
| website = https://wakura-omatsuri.com/
}}
'''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall''' (和倉温泉お祭り会館, ''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Kaikan'') is a cultural museum in [[Nanao City]], [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan. Located in the famous [[Wakura Onsen]] hot spring area, the museum showcases the city's four major traditional festivals, including large-scale {{ill|Yatai (festival floats)|ja|山車}} and other festival artifacts.
== Overview ==
Nanao City, known for its numerous traditional festivals, decided to establish the museum following the [[2016]] inclusion of the {{ill|Seihakusai Hikiyama Festival|ja|青柏祭}} as a [[UNESCO]] [[Intangible Cultural Heritage]]. The facility was developed by renovating the aging 1981 Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall, addressing structural issues and converting the main hall into an exhibition space.<ref name="Hokkoku">Hokkoku Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Hokuriku">Hokuriku Chunichi Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Furusato">[https://www.furusato-tax.jp/gcf/493 Furusato Tax Project for Festival Preservation]</ref>
The facility highlights four key festivals:
* [[Seihakusai]] - designated as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Okuma Kabuto Festival]] - also a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Mukōda Fire Festival]] - recognized as a [[Prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] and part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
* [[Ishizaki Hōtō Festival]] - part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
Initially slated for an April 25, 2020, opening, the launch was delayed due to the COVID-19 pandemic.<ref name="Hokkoku"/>
== Features ==
=== Exhibition Hall (Paid Admission)
The former 1,100-seat main hall has been transformed into a 17-meter-high exhibition space displaying full-scale festival floats and artifacts.<ref>[http://www.ryoko-net.co.jp/?p=78090 Ryoko Shimbun]</ref> Exhibits include:
* Seihakusai's "Dekayama" - a new full-scale replica created as a fourth float for permanent display.
* Okuma Kabuto's Waku Bata - 16 meters high.
* Mukōda Fire Festival’s Ōtaimatsu - represented by a 15-meter banner due to the original’s 30-meter height.
* Ishizaki Hōtō’s Daifutō - 13 meters high.
=== === Festival Theater and Interactive Zone === The theater features a 16.2-meter-wide screen showing immersive festival footage. Interactive experiences allow visitors to "participate" in festivals through projections and simulations.<ref name="Hokuriku"/>
=== === Tourism Information Entrance (Free Zone) === The entrance area features:
* Miniature and small-scale exhibits of local floats.
* Tourism information and guides.
* A gift shop offering festival-themed souvenirs.
=== === Additional Facilities === * Meeting rooms and Japanese-style rooms (available for rent).
* Cooking practice rooms.
== Visitor Information ==
* Open: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM (last entry at 4:30 PM)
* Closed: Second and fourth Wednesdays (or following day if a holiday) and during New Year’s holidays.
* Admission: Adults ¥800, children ¥400, infants free.
* Free parking available for buses and private vehicles.
== Access ==
* '''By car''': ~6 minutes from [[Wakura IC]] on the [[Noto Satoyama Kaidō]].
* '''By train''': ~20 minutes on foot or 6 minutes by car from [[Wakura Onsen Station]].
* '''By bus''': Immediate access from "Omatsuri Kaikan" bus stop (Hokutetsu Noto Bus).
== Surrounding Attractions ==
* [[Wakura Onsen]]
* Wakura Onsen Sōyu Public Bath
* [[Wakura Showa Museum and Toy Museum]]
* [[Isolite Diatomite Memorial Museum]]
* [[Yoshihiro Tsujiguchi Art Museum]]
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== External Links ==
* {{Official website|https://wakura-omatsuri.com/|Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall}} [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Wakura Onsen Festival Hall}} [[Category:Peripheral articles]] [[Category:qq-Wakura Onsen Festival Hall-qq]] [[Category:Wakura Onsen Festival Hall]][[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Wakura Onsen Festival Hall}} [[Category:qq-Wakura Onsen Festival Hall-qq]] [[Category:Wakura Onsen Festival Hall]]{{citation needed|Wakura Onsen Festival Hall}}[[Category:Museums in Ishikawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Wakura Onsen Festival Hall}} [[Category:qq-Wakura Onsen Festival Hall-qq]] [[Category:Wakura Onsen Festival Hall]][[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Wakura Onsen Festival Hall}} [[Category:qq-Wakura Onsen Festival Hall-qq]] [[Category:Wakura Onsen Festival Hall]]{{citation needed|Wakura Onsen Festival Hall}}[[Category:Japanese folklore museums]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Wakura Onsen Festival Hall}} [[Category:qq-Wakura Onsen Festival Hall-qq]] [[Category:Wakura Onsen Festival Hall]][[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Wakura Onsen Festival Hall}} [[Category:qq-Wakura Onsen Festival Hall-qq]] [[Category:Wakura Onsen Festival Hall]]{{citation needed|Wakura Onsen Festival Hall}}[[Category:Nanao, Ishikawa]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Wakura Onsen Festival Hall}} [[Category:qq-Wakura Onsen Festival Hall-qq]] [[Category:Wakura Onsen Festival Hall]][[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Wakura Onsen Festival Hall}} [[Category:qq-Wakura Onsen Festival Hall-qq]] [[Category:Wakura Onsen Festival Hall]]{{citation needed|Wakura Onsen Festival Hall}}

| owner = [[Nanao City]]
| operator = Nanao-Nakanoto DMO
| established = [[June 1, 2020]]
| location = 13-1 Wakuramachi 2-bu, Nanao City, [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan
| coordinates = {{coord|37|5|11.67|N|136|55|7.49|E|display=inline,title}}
| website = https://wakura-omatsuri.com/
}}
'''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall''' (和倉温泉お祭り会館, ''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Kaikan'') is a cultural museum in [[Nanao City]], [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan. Located in the famous [[Wakura Onsen]] hot spring area, the museum showcases the city's four major traditional festivals, including large-scale {{ill|Yatai (festival floats)|ja|山車}} and other festival artifacts.
== Overview ==
Nanao City, known for its numerous traditional festivals, decided to establish the museum following the [[2016]] inclusion of the {{ill|Seihakusai Hikiyama Festival|ja|青柏祭}} as a [[UNESCO]] [[Intangible Cultural Heritage]]. The facility was developed by renovating the aging 1981 Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall, addressing structural issues and converting the main hall into an exhibition space.<ref name="Hokkoku">Hokkoku Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Hokuriku">Hokuriku Chunichi Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Furusato">[https://www.furusato-tax.jp/gcf/493 Furusato Tax Project for Festival Preservation]</ref>
The facility highlights four key festivals:
* [[Seihakusai]] - designated as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Okuma Kabuto Festival]] - also a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Mukōda Fire Festival]] - recognized as a [[Prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] and part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
* [[Ishizaki Hōtō Festival]] - part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
Initially slated for an April 25, 2020, opening, the launch was delayed due to the COVID-19 pandemic.<ref name="Hokkoku"/>
== Features ==
=== Exhibition Hall (Paid Admission)
The former 1,100-seat main hall has been transformed into a 17-meter-high exhibition space displaying full-scale festival floats and artifacts.<ref>[http://www.ryoko-net.co.jp/?p=78090 Ryoko Shimbun]</ref> Exhibits include:
* Seihakusai's "Dekayama" - a new full-scale replica created as a fourth float for permanent display.
* Okuma Kabuto's Waku Bata - 16 meters high.
* Mukōda Fire Festival’s Ōtaimatsu - represented by a 15-meter banner due to the original’s 30-meter height.
* Ishizaki Hōtō’s Daifutō - 13 meters high.
=== === Festival Theater and Interactive Zone === The theater features a 16.2-meter-wide screen showing immersive festival footage. Interactive experiences allow visitors to "participate" in festivals through projections and simulations.<ref name="Hokuriku"/>
=== === Tourism Information Entrance (Free Zone) === The entrance area features:
* Miniature and small-scale exhibits of local floats.
* Tourism information and guides.
* A gift shop offering festival-themed souvenirs.
=== === Additional Facilities === * Meeting rooms and Japanese-style rooms (available for rent).
* Cooking practice rooms.
== Visitor Information ==
* Open: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM (last entry at 4:30 PM)
* Closed: Second and fourth Wednesdays (or following day if a holiday) and during New Year’s holidays.
* Admission: Adults ¥800, children ¥400, infants free.
* Free parking available for buses and private vehicles.
== Access ==
* '''By car''': ~6 minutes from [[Wakura IC]] on the [[Noto Satoyama Kaidō]].
* '''By train''': ~20 minutes on foot or 6 minutes by car from [[Wakura Onsen Station]].
* '''By bus''': Immediate access from "Omatsuri Kaikan" bus stop (Hokutetsu Noto Bus).
== Surrounding Attractions ==
* [[Wakura Onsen]]
* Wakura Onsen Sōyu Public Bath
* [[Wakura Showa Museum and Toy Museum]]
* [[Isolite Diatomite Memorial Museum]]
* [[Yoshihiro Tsujiguchi Art Museum]]

{{Referencing style|date=May 2021}}
{{Museum
| name = Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall
| native_name = 和倉温泉お祭り会館
| native_name_lang = ja
| image =
| former_name = Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall
| focus = Folklore, History
| director =
| owner = [[Nanao City]]
| operator = Nanao-Nakanoto DMO
| established = [[June 1, 2020]]
| location = 13-1 Wakuramachi 2-bu, Nanao City, [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan
| coordinates = {{coord|37|5|11.67|N|136|55|7.49|E|display=inline,title}}
| website = https://wakura-omatsuri.com/
}}
'''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall''' (和倉温泉お祭り会館, ''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Kaikan'') is a cultural museum in [[Nanao City]], [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan. Located in the famous [[Wakura Onsen]] hot spring area, the museum showcases the city's four major traditional festivals, including large-scale {{ill|Yatai (festival floats)|ja|山車}} and other festival artifacts.
== Overview ==
Nanao City, known for its numerous traditional festivals, decided to establish the museum following the [[2016]] inclusion of the {{ill|Seihakusai Hikiyama Festival|ja|青柏祭}} as a [[UNESCO]] [[Intangible Cultural Heritage]]. The facility was developed by renovating the aging 1981 Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall, addressing structural issues and converting the main hall into an exhibition space.<ref name="Hokkoku">Hokkoku Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Hokuriku">Hokuriku Chunichi Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Furusato">[https://www.furusato-tax.jp/gcf/493 Furusato Tax Project for Festival Preservation]</ref>
The facility highlights four key festivals:
* [[Seihakusai]] - designated as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Okuma Kabuto Festival]] - also a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Mukōda Fire Festival]] - recognized as a [[Prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] and part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
* [[Ishizaki Hōtō Festival]] - part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
Initially slated for an April 25, 2020, opening, the launch was delayed due to the COVID-19 pandemic.<ref name="Hokkoku"/>
== Features ==
=== Exhibition Hall (Paid Admission)
The former 1,100-seat main hall has been transformed into a 17-meter-high exhibition space displaying full-scale festival floats and artifacts.<ref>[http://www.ryoko-net.co.jp/?p=78090 Ryoko Shimbun]</ref> Exhibits include:
* Seihakusai's "Dekayama" - a new full-scale replica created as a fourth float for permanent display.
* Okuma Kabuto's Waku Bata - 16 meters high.
* Mukōda Fire Festival’s Ōtaimatsu - represented by a 15-meter banner due to the original’s 30-meter height.
* Ishizaki Hōtō’s Daifutō - 13 meters high.
=== === Festival Theater and Interactive Zone === The theater features a 16.2-meter-wide screen showing immersive festival footage. Interactive experiences allow visitors to "participate" in festivals through projections and simulations.<ref name="Hokuriku"/>
=== === Tourism Information Entrance (Free Zone) === The entrance area features:
* Miniature and small-scale exhibits of local floats.
* Tourism information and guides.
* A gift shop offering festival-themed souvenirs.
=== === Additional Facilities === * Meeting rooms and Japanese-style rooms (available for rent).
* Cooking practice rooms.
== Visitor Information ==
* Open: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM (last entry at 4:30 PM)
* Closed: Second and fourth Wednesdays (or following day if a holiday) and during New Year’s holidays.
* Admission: Adults ¥800, children ¥400, infants free.
* Free parking available for buses and private vehicles.
== Access ==
* '''By car''': ~6 minutes from [[Wakura IC]] on the [[Noto Satoyama Kaidō]].
* '''By train''': ~20 minutes on foot or 6 minutes by car from [[Wakura Onsen Station]].
* '''By bus''': Immediate access from "Omatsuri Kaikan" bus stop (Hokutetsu Noto Bus).
== Surrounding Attractions ==
* [[Wakura Onsen]]
* Wakura Onsen Sōyu Public Bath
* [[Wakura Showa Museum and Toy Museum]]
* [[Isolite Diatomite Memorial Museum]]
* [[Yoshihiro Tsujiguchi Art Museum]]

{{Reflist}}
== External Links ==

revision.text:
{{Referencing style|date=May 2021}}
{{Museum
| name = Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall
| native_name = 和倉温泉お祭り会館
| native_name_lang = ja
| image =
| former_name = Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall
| focus = Folklore, History
| director =
| owner = [[Nanao City]]
| operator = Nanao-Nakanoto DMO
| established = [[June 1, 2020]]
| location = 13-1 Wakuramachi 2-bu, Nanao City, [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan
| coordinates = {{coord|37|5|11.67|N|136|55|7.49|E|display=inline,title}}
| website = https://wakura-omatsuri.com/
}}
'''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall''' (和倉温泉お祭り会館, ''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Kaikan'') is a cultural museum in [[Nanao City]], [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan. Located in the famous [[Wakura Onsen]] hot spring area, the museum showcases the city's four major traditional festivals, including large-scale {{ill|Yatai (festival floats)|ja|山車}} and other festival artifacts.
== Overview ==
Nanao City, known for its numerous traditional festivals, decided to establish the museum following the [[2016]] inclusion of the {{ill|Seihakusai Hikiyama Festival|ja|青柏祭}} as a [[UNESCO]] [[Intangible Cultural Heritage]]. The facility was developed by renovating the aging 1981 Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall, addressing structural issues and converting the main hall into an exhibition space.<ref name="Hokkoku">Hokkoku Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Hokuriku">Hokuriku Chunichi Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Furusato">[https://www.furusato-tax.jp/gcf/493 Furusato Tax Project for Festival Preservation]</ref>
The facility highlights four key festivals:
* [[Seihakusai]] - designated as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Okuma Kabuto Festival]] - also a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Mukōda Fire Festival]] - recognized as a [[Prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] and part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
* [[Ishizaki Hōtō Festival]] - part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
Initially slated for an April 25, 2020, opening, the launch was delayed due to the COVID-19 pandemic.<ref name="Hokkoku"/>
== Features ==
=== Exhibition Hall (Paid Admission)
The former 1,100-seat main hall has been transformed into a 17-meter-high exhibition space displaying full-scale festival floats and artifacts.<ref>[http://www.ryoko-net.co.jp/?p=78090 Ryoko Shimbun]</ref> Exhibits include:
* Seihakusai's "Dekayama" - a new full-scale replica created as a fourth float for permanent display.
* Okuma Kabuto's Waku Bata - 16 meters high.
* Mukōda Fire Festival’s Ōtaimatsu - represented by a 15-meter banner due to the original’s 30-meter height.
* Ishizaki Hōtō’s Daifutō - 13 meters high.
=== === Festival Theater and Interactive Zone === The theater features a 16.2-meter-wide screen showing immersive festival footage. Interactive experiences allow visitors to "participate" in festivals through projections and simulations.<ref name="Hokuriku"/>
=== === Tourism Information Entrance (Free Zone) === The entrance area features:
* Miniature and small-scale exhibits of local floats.
* Tourism information and guides.
* A gift shop offering festival-themed souvenirs.
=== === Additional Facilities === * Meeting rooms and Japanese-style rooms (available for rent).
* Cooking practice rooms.
== Visitor Information ==
* Open: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM (last entry at 4:30 PM)
* Closed: Second and fourth Wednesdays (or following day if a holiday) and during New Year’s holidays.
* Admission: Adults ¥800, children ¥400, infants free.
* Free parking available for buses and private vehicles.
== Access ==
* '''By car''': ~6 minutes from [[Wakura IC]] on the [[Noto Satoyama Kaidō]].
* '''By train''': ~20 minutes on foot or 6 minutes by car from [[Wakura Onsen Station]].
* '''By bus''': Immediate access from "Omatsuri Kaikan" bus stop (Hokutetsu Noto Bus).
== Surrounding Attractions ==
* [[Wakura Onsen]]
* Wakura Onsen Sōyu Public Bath
* [[Wakura Showa Museum and Toy Museum]]
* [[Isolite Diatomite Memorial Museum]]
* [[Yoshihiro Tsujiguchi Art Museum]]
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== External Links ==

The former 1,100-seat main hall has been transformed into a 17-meter-high exhibition space displaying full-scale festival floats and artifacts.<ref>[http://www.ryoko-net.co.jp/?p=78090 Ryoko Shimbun]</ref> Exhibits include:

* Ishizaki Hōtō’s Daifutō - 13 meters high.

=== === Tourism Information Entrance (Free Zone) === The entrance area features:
* Miniature and small-scale exhibits of local floats.
* Tourism information and guides.
* A gift shop offering festival-themed souvenirs.
=== === Additional Facilities === * Meeting rooms and Japanese-style rooms (available for rent).
* Cooking practice rooms.
== Visitor Information ==
* Open: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM (last entry at 4:30 PM)
* Closed: Second and fourth Wednesdays (or following day if a holiday) and during New Year’s holidays.

* Free parking available for buses and private vehicles.

| website = https://wakura-omatsuri.com/
}}
'''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall''' (和倉温泉お祭り会館, ''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Kaikan'') is a cultural museum in [[Nanao City]], [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan. Located in the famous [[Wakura Onsen]] hot spring area, the museum showcases the city's four major traditional festivals, including large-scale {{ill|Yatai (festival floats)|ja|山車}} and other festival artifacts.
== Overview ==
Nanao City, known for its numerous traditional festivals, decided to establish the museum following the [[2016]] inclusion of the {{ill|Seihakusai Hikiyama Festival|ja|青柏祭}} as a [[UNESCO]] [[Intangible Cultural Heritage]]. The facility was developed by renovating the aging 1981 Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall, addressing structural issues and converting the main hall into an exhibition space.<ref name="Hokkoku">Hokkoku Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Hokuriku">Hokuriku Chunichi Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Furusato">[https://www.furusato-tax.jp/gcf/493 Furusato Tax Project for Festival Preservation]</ref>
The facility highlights four key festivals:
* [[Seihakusai]] - designated as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Okuma Kabuto Festival]] - also a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Mukōda Fire Festival]] - recognized as a [[Prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] and part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
* [[Ishizaki Hōtō Festival]] - part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
Initially slated for an April 25, 2020, opening, the launch was delayed due to the COVID-19 pandemic.<ref name="Hokkoku"/>
== Features ==
=== Exhibition Hall (Paid Admission)
The former 1,100-seat main hall has been transformed into a 17-meter-high exhibition space displaying full-scale festival floats and artifacts.<ref>[http://www.ryoko-net.co.jp/?p=78090 Ryoko Shimbun]</ref> Exhibits include:
* Seihakusai's "Dekayama" - a new full-scale replica created as a fourth float for permanent display.
* Okuma Kabuto's Waku Bata - 16 meters high.
* Mukōda Fire Festival’s Ōtaimatsu - represented by a 15-meter banner due to the original’s 30-meter height.
* Ishizaki Hōtō’s Daifutō - 13 meters high.
=== === Festival Theater and Interactive Zone === The theater features a 16.2-meter-wide screen showing immersive festival footage. Interactive experiences allow visitors to "participate" in festivals through projections and simulations.<ref name="Hokuriku"/>
=== === Tourism Information Entrance (Free Zone) === The entrance area features:
* Miniature and small-scale exhibits of local floats.
* Tourism information and guides.
* A gift shop offering festival-themed souvenirs.
=== === Additional Facilities === * Meeting rooms and Japanese-style rooms (available for rent).
* Cooking practice rooms.
== Visitor Information ==
* Open: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM (last entry at 4:30 PM)
* Closed: Second and fourth Wednesdays (or following day if a holiday) and during New Year’s holidays.
* Admission: Adults ¥800, children ¥400, infants free.
* Free parking available for buses and private vehicles.
== Access ==
* '''By car''': ~6 minutes from [[Wakura IC]] on the [[Noto Satoyama Kaidō]].
* '''By train''': ~20 minutes on foot or 6 minutes by car from [[Wakura Onsen Station]].
* '''By bus''': Immediate access from "Omatsuri Kaikan" bus stop (Hokutetsu Noto Bus).
== Surrounding Attractions ==
* [[Wakura Onsen]]
* Wakura Onsen Sōyu Public Bath
* [[Wakura Showa Museum and Toy Museum]]
* [[Isolite Diatomite Memorial Museum]]
* [[Yoshihiro Tsujiguchi Art Museum]]

{{Referencing style|date=May 2021}}
{{Museum
| name = Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall
| native_name = 和倉温泉お祭り会館
| native_name_lang = ja
| image =
| former_name = Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall
| focus = Folklore, History
| director =
| owner = [[Nanao City]]
| operator = Nanao-Nakanoto DMO
| established = [[June 1, 2020]]
| location = 13-1 Wakuramachi 2-bu, Nanao City, [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan
| coordinates = {{coord|37|5|11.67|N|136|55|7.49|E|display=inline,title}}
| website = https://wakura-omatsuri.com/
}}
'''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall''' (和倉温泉お祭り会館, ''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Kaikan'') is a cultural museum in [[Nanao City]], [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan. Located in the famous [[Wakura Onsen]] hot spring area, the museum showcases the city's four major traditional festivals, including large-scale {{ill|Yatai (festival floats)|ja|山車}} and other festival artifacts.
== Overview ==
Nanao City, known for its numerous traditional festivals, decided to establish the museum following the [[2016]] inclusion of the {{ill|Seihakusai Hikiyama Festival|ja|青柏祭}} as a [[UNESCO]] [[Intangible Cultural Heritage]]. The facility was developed by renovating the aging 1981 Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall, addressing structural issues and converting the main hall into an exhibition space.<ref name="Hokkoku">Hokkoku Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Hokuriku">Hokuriku Chunichi Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Furusato">[https://www.furusato-tax.jp/gcf/493 Furusato Tax Project for Festival Preservation]</ref>
The facility highlights four key festivals:
* [[Seihakusai]] - designated as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Okuma Kabuto Festival]] - also a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Mukōda Fire Festival]] - recognized as a [[Prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] and part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
* [[Ishizaki Hōtō Festival]] - part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
Initially slated for an April 25, 2020, opening, the launch was delayed due to the COVID-19 pandemic.<ref name="Hokkoku"/>
== Features ==
=== Exhibition Hall (Paid Admission)
The former 1,100-seat main hall has been transformed into a 17-meter-high exhibition space displaying full-scale festival floats and artifacts.<ref>[http://www.ryoko-net.co.jp/?p=78090 Ryoko Shimbun]</ref> Exhibits include:
* Seihakusai's "Dekayama" - a new full-scale replica created as a fourth float for permanent display.
* Okuma Kabuto's Waku Bata - 16 meters high.
* Mukōda Fire Festival’s Ōtaimatsu - represented by a 15-meter banner due to the original’s 30-meter height.
* Ishizaki Hōtō’s Daifutō - 13 meters high.
=== === Festival Theater and Interactive Zone === The theater features a 16.2-meter-wide screen showing immersive festival footage. Interactive experiences allow visitors to "participate" in festivals through projections and simulations.<ref name="Hokuriku"/>
=== === Tourism Information Entrance (Free Zone) === The entrance area features:
* Miniature and small-scale exhibits of local floats.
* Tourism information and guides.
* A gift shop offering festival-themed souvenirs.
=== === Additional Facilities === * Meeting rooms and Japanese-style rooms (available for rent).
* Cooking practice rooms.
== Visitor Information ==
* Open: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM (last entry at 4:30 PM)
* Closed: Second and fourth Wednesdays (or following day if a holiday) and during New Year’s holidays.
* Admission: Adults ¥800, children ¥400, infants free.
* Free parking available for buses and private vehicles.
== Access ==
* '''By car''': ~6 minutes from [[Wakura IC]] on the [[Noto Satoyama Kaidō]].
* '''By train''': ~20 minutes on foot or 6 minutes by car from [[Wakura Onsen Station]].
* '''By bus''': Immediate access from "Omatsuri Kaikan" bus stop (Hokutetsu Noto Bus).
== Surrounding Attractions ==
* [[Wakura Onsen]]
* Wakura Onsen Sōyu Public Bath
* [[Wakura Showa Museum and Toy Museum]]
* [[Isolite Diatomite Memorial Museum]]
* [[Yoshihiro Tsujiguchi Art Museum]]

{{Reflist}}
== External Links ==

{{Referencing style|date=May 2021}}
{{Museum
| name = Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall
| native_name = 和倉温泉お祭り会館
| native_name_lang = ja
| image =
| former_name = Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall
| focus = Folklore, History
| director =
| owner = [[Nanao City]]
| operator = Nanao-Nakanoto DMO
| established = [[June 1, 2020]]
| location = 13-1 Wakuramachi 2-bu, Nanao City, [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan
| coordinates = {{coord|37|5|11.67|N|136|55|7.49|E|display=inline,title}}
| website = https://wakura-omatsuri.com/
}}
'''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall''' (和倉温泉お祭り会館, ''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Kaikan'') is a cultural museum in [[Nanao City]], [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan. Located in the famous [[Wakura Onsen]] hot spring area, the museum showcases the city's four major traditional festivals, including large-scale {{ill|Yatai (festival floats)|ja|山車}} and other festival artifacts.
== Overview ==
Nanao City, known for its numerous traditional festivals, decided to establish the museum following the [[2016]] inclusion of the {{ill|Seihakusai Hikiyama Festival|ja|青柏祭}} as a [[UNESCO]] [[Intangible Cultural Heritage]]. The facility was developed by renovating the aging 1981 Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall, addressing structural issues and converting the main hall into an exhibition space.<ref name="Hokkoku">Hokkoku Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Hokuriku">Hokuriku Chunichi Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Furusato">[https://www.furusato-tax.jp/gcf/493 Furusato Tax Project for Festival Preservation]</ref>
The facility highlights four key festivals:
* [[Seihakusai]] - designated as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Okuma Kabuto Festival]] - also a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Mukōda Fire Festival]] - recognized as a [[Prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] and part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
* [[Ishizaki Hōtō Festival]] - part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
Initially slated for an April 25, 2020, opening, the launch was delayed due to the COVID-19 pandemic.<ref name="Hokkoku"/>
== Features ==
=== Exhibition Hall (Paid Admission)
The former 1,100-seat main hall has been transformed into a 17-meter-high exhibition space displaying full-scale festival floats and artifacts.<ref>[http://www.ryoko-net.co.jp/?p=78090 Ryoko Shimbun]</ref> Exhibits include:
* Seihakusai's "Dekayama" - a new full-scale replica created as a fourth float for permanent display.
* Okuma Kabuto's Waku Bata - 16 meters high.
* Mukōda Fire Festival’s Ōtaimatsu - represented by a 15-meter banner due to the original’s 30-meter height.
* Ishizaki Hōtō’s Daifutō - 13 meters high.
=== === Festival Theater and Interactive Zone === The theater features a 16.2-meter-wide screen showing immersive festival footage. Interactive experiences allow visitors to "participate" in festivals through projections and simulations.<ref name="Hokuriku"/>
=== === Tourism Information Entrance (Free Zone) === The entrance area features:
* Miniature and small-scale exhibits of local floats.
* Tourism information and guides.
* A gift shop offering festival-themed souvenirs.
=== === Additional Facilities === * Meeting rooms and Japanese-style rooms (available for rent).
* Cooking practice rooms.
== Visitor Information ==
* Open: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM (last entry at 4:30 PM)
* Closed: Second and fourth Wednesdays (or following day if a holiday) and during New Year’s holidays.
* Admission: Adults ¥800, children ¥400, infants free.
* Free parking available for buses and private vehicles.
== Access ==
* '''By car''': ~6 minutes from [[Wakura IC]] on the [[Noto Satoyama Kaidō]].
* '''By train''': ~20 minutes on foot or 6 minutes by car from [[Wakura Onsen Station]].
* '''By bus''': Immediate access from "Omatsuri Kaikan" bus stop (Hokutetsu Noto Bus).
== Surrounding Attractions ==
* [[Wakura Onsen]]
* Wakura Onsen Sōyu Public Bath
* [[Wakura Showa Museum and Toy Museum]]
* [[Isolite Diatomite Memorial Museum]]
* [[Yoshihiro Tsujiguchi Art Museum]]
== References ==
{{Reflist}}
== External Links ==

* [[Seihakusai]] - designated as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Okuma Kabuto Festival]] - also a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Mukōda Fire Festival]] - recognized as a [[Prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] and part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
* [[Ishizaki Hōtō Festival]] - part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
Initially slated for an April 25, 2020, opening, the launch was delayed due to the COVID-19 pandemic.<ref name="Hokkoku"/>

'''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall''' (和倉温泉お祭り会館, ''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Kaikan'') is a cultural museum in [[Nanao City]], [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan. Located in the famous [[Wakura Onsen]] hot spring area, the museum showcases the city's four major traditional festivals, including large-scale {{ill|Yatai (festival floats)|ja|山車}} and other festival artifacts.
== Overview ==
Nanao City, known for its numerous traditional festivals, decided to establish the museum following the [[2016]] inclusion of the {{ill|Seihakusai Hikiyama Festival|ja|青柏祭}} as a [[UNESCO]] [[Intangible Cultural Heritage]]. The facility was developed by renovating the aging 1981 Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall, addressing structural issues and converting the main hall into an exhibition space.<ref name="Hokkoku">Hokkoku Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Hokuriku">Hokuriku Chunichi Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Furusato">[https://www.furusato-tax.jp/gcf/493 Furusato Tax Project for Festival Preservation]</ref>
The facility highlights four key festivals:
* [[Seihakusai]] - designated as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Okuma Kabuto Festival]] - also a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Mukōda Fire Festival]] - recognized as a [[Prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] and part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
* [[Ishizaki Hōtō Festival]] - part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
Initially slated for an April 25, 2020, opening, the launch was delayed due to the COVID-19 pandemic.<ref name="Hokkoku"/>
== Features ==
=== Exhibition Hall (Paid Admission)===
The former 1,100-seat main hall has been transformed into a 17-meter-high exhibition space displaying full-scale festival floats and artifacts.<ref>[http://www.ryoko-net.co.jp/?p=78090 Ryoko Shimbun]</ref> Exhibits include:
* Seihakusai's "Dekayama" - a new full-scale replica created as a fourth float for permanent display.
* Okuma Kabuto's Waku Bata - 16 meters high.
* Mukōda Fire Festival’s Ōtaimatsu - represented by a 15-meter banner due to the original’s 30-meter height.
* Ishizaki Hōtō’s Daifutō - 13 meters high.
=== Festival Theater and Interactive Zone === The theater features a 16.2-meter-wide screen showing immersive festival footage. Interactive experiences allow visitors to "participate" in festivals through projections and simulations.<ref name="Hokuriku"/>
=== Tourism Information Entrance (Free Zone) === The entrance area features:
* Miniature and small-scale exhibits of local floats.
* Tourism information and guides.
* A gift shop offering festival-themed souvenirs.
=== Additional Facilities === * Meeting rooms and Japanese-style rooms (available for rent).
* Cooking practice rooms.
== Visitor Information ==
* Open: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM (last entry at 4:30 PM)
* Closed: Second and fourth Wednesdays (or following day if a holiday) and during New Year’s holidays.
* Admission: Adults ¥800, children ¥400, infants free.
* Free parking available for buses and private vehicles.
== Access ==
* '''By car''': ~6 minutes from [[Wakura IC]] on the [[Noto Satoyama Kaidō]].
* '''By train''': ~20 minutes on foot or 6 minutes by car from [[Wakura Onsen Station]].
* '''By bus''': Immediate access from "Omatsuri Kaikan" bus stop (Hokutetsu Noto Bus).
== Surrounding Attractions ==
* [[Wakura Onsen]]
* Wakura Onsen Sōyu Public Bath
* [[Wakura Showa Museum and Toy Museum]]
* [[Isolite Diatomite Memorial Museum]]
* [[Yoshihiro Tsujiguchi Art Museum]]

{{Referencing style|date=May 2021}}
{{Museum
| name = Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall
| native_name = 和倉温泉お祭り会館
| native_name_lang = ja
| image =
| former_name = Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall
| focus = Folklore, History
| director =
| owner = [[Nanao City]]
| operator = Nanao-Nakanoto DMO
| established = [[June 1, 2020]]
| location = 13-1 Wakuramachi 2-bu, Nanao City, [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan
| coordinates = {{coord|37|5|11.67|N|136|55|7.49|E|display=inline,title}}
| website = https://wakura-omatsuri.com/
}}
'''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Hall''' (和倉温泉お祭り会館, ''Wakura Onsen Omatsuri Kaikan'') is a cultural museum in [[Nanao City]], [[Ishikawa Prefecture]], Japan. Located in the famous [[Wakura Onsen]] hot spring area, the museum showcases the city's four major traditional festivals, including large-scale {{ill|Yatai (festival floats)|ja|山車}} and other festival artifacts.
== Overview ==
Nanao City, known for its numerous traditional festivals, decided to establish the museum following the [[2016]] inclusion of the {{ill|Seihakusai Hikiyama Festival|ja|青柏祭}} as a [[UNESCO]] [[Intangible Cultural Heritage]]. The facility was developed by renovating the aging 1981 Wakura Onsen Tourism Hall, addressing structural issues and converting the main hall into an exhibition space.<ref name="Hokkoku">Hokkoku Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Hokuriku">Hokuriku Chunichi Shimbun, June 1, 2020.</ref><ref name="Furusato">[https://www.furusato-tax.jp/gcf/493 Furusato Tax Project for Festival Preservation]</ref>
The facility highlights four key festivals:
* [[Seihakusai]] - designated as a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Okuma Kabuto Festival]] - also a National Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property
* [[Mukōda Fire Festival]] - recognized as a [[Prefectural Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] and part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
* [[Ishizaki Hōtō Festival]] - part of the "Noto Kiriko Festivals"
Initially slated for an April 25, 2020, opening, the launch was delayed due to the COVID-19 pandemic.<ref name="Hokkoku"/>
== Features ==
=== Exhibition Hall (Paid Admission)===
The former 1,100-seat main hall has been transformed into a 17-meter-high exhibition space displaying full-scale festival floats and artifacts.<ref>[http://www.ryoko-net.co.jp/?p=78090 Ryoko Shimbun]</ref> Exhibits include:
* Seihakusai's "Dekayama" - a new full-scale replica created as a fourth float for permanent display.
* Okuma Kabuto's Waku Bata - 16 meters high.
* Mukōda Fire Festival’s Ōtaimatsu - represented by a 15-meter banner due to the original’s 30-meter height.
* Ishizaki Hōtō’s Daifutō - 13 meters high.
=== Festival Theater and Interactive Zone === The theater features a 16.2-meter-wide screen showing immersive festival footage. Interactive experiences allow visitors to "participate" in festivals through projections and simulations.<ref name="Hokuriku"/>
=== Tourism Information Entrance (Free Zone) === The entrance area features:
* Miniature and small-scale exhibits of local floats.
* Tourism information and guides.
* A gift shop offering festival-themed souvenirs.
=== Additional Facilities === * Meeting rooms and Japanese-style rooms (available for rent).
* Cooking practice rooms.
== Visitor Information ==
* Open: 9:00 AM - 5:00 PM (last entry at 4:30 PM)
* Closed: Second and fourth Wednesdays (or following day if a holiday) and during New Year’s holidays.
* Admission: Adults ¥800, children ¥400, infants free.
* Free parking available for buses and private vehicles.
== Access ==
* '''By car''': ~6 minutes from [[Wakura IC]] on the [[Noto Satoyama Kaidō]].
* '''By train''': ~20 minutes on foot or 6 minutes by car from [[Wakura Onsen Station]].
* '''By bus''': Immediate access from "Omatsuri Kaikan" bus stop (Hokutetsu Noto Bus).
== Surrounding Attractions ==
* [[Wakura Onsen]]
* Wakura Onsen Sōyu Public Bath
* [[Wakura Showa Museum and Toy Museum]]
* [[Isolite Diatomite Memorial Museum]]
* [[Yoshihiro Tsujiguchi Art Museum]]

{{Reflist}}
== External Links ==
